industry.headline: SECI Issues Transformer Tender for 700 MW Solar Project
industry.summary: The Solar Energy Corporation of India (SECI) has invited bids for engineering, design, manufacturing, testing, and supply of five 160 MVA, 220/33-33 KV power transformers for a 700 MW solar project at Radhanesda in Gujarat. The tender includes specifications for technical parameters and compliance. Successful bidders will deliver transformers per agreed terms. This procurement aims to expand solar infrastructure in India.

tech.headline: Chinese researchers achieve 33.33% efficiency in tandem solar cells
tech.summary: CAS-led researchers developed a peak-selective passivation strategy using aluminum oxide nanocoating to address leakage in pyramid-textured silicon for perovskite-silicon tandem cells. The method is compatible with existing silicon manufacturing and enables high-efficiency, stable devices reaching around 33% efficiency.

learn.title: Building Integrated Photovoltaics (BIPV)
learn.intro: BIPV turns building elements—roofs, facades, windows—into power generators, merging architecture with clean electricity. This approach cuts material costs, reduces carbon footprints, and creates aesthetically pleasing energy solutions essential for dense urban growth.
learn.c1_text: Imagine a skyscraper’s glass curtain wall acting like a giant solar panel. A 100‑m² façade can produce roughly 15 kWh per day, enough to run a small office. The electricity is generated where it is needed, cutting transmission losses.
learn.c2_text: BIPV must meet building codes, fire safety, and structural loads. Modules are often laminated into shingle‑size pieces, requiring careful design of wiring and inverters so that the building’s aesthetic isn’t compromised while maintaining grid‑ready output.
learn.c3_text: India’s Ministry of New & Renewable Energy incentivises BIPV through the “Solar on Buildings” scheme, targeting 5 GW of BIPV by 2030. Pilot projects in Delhi and Bengaluru are installing PV glass on office towers, reducing peak‑hour demand by up to 20 %.
learn.stat2: BIPV market projected to reach $12 bn globally by 2028
learn.stat3: Typical BIPV roof yields 120 kWh / m² / year
learn.stat4: US DOE reports 30 % cost reduction vs. classic PV when integrated

learn.title: The Duck Curve Challenge
learn.intro: As solar output peaks midday, the grid sees a steep drop in net demand, followed by a rapid climb at sunset. This “duck curve” stresses flexible resources and can cause reliability issues if not managed properly.
learn.c1_text: Picture a duck silhouette where the belly is the midday low net load. In California 2023, net demand fell 30 % at 2 p.m. then surged 15 % within two hours after sunset, demanding fast‑ramping generators.
learn.c2_text: Battery storage, demand‑response, and fast‑start gas turbines can fill the evening gap. A 1 GW battery can shave 5 % of the peak‑hour ramp, smoothing the curve and avoiding curtailment of solar.
learn.c3_text: India’s solar share reached 15 % of total generation in 2024, creating a modest duck curve in western states. The Central Electricity Regulatory Commission encourages 5 GW of pumped‑hydro and battery projects to mitigate evening ramps.

learn.title: Smart Grids & Advanced Metering
learn.intro: Smart grids embed sensors, communication, and automation into the power network, enabling two‑way flows of electricity and information. Advanced metering empowers consumers and operators to balance supply and demand in real time.
learn.c1_text: Think of the grid as a highway with traffic lights that adjust instantly. Smart sensors report voltage and load every few seconds, allowing operators to redirect power like dynamic lane changes, reducing outages by up to 30 %.
learn.c2_text: A smart meter shows real‑time usage on a smartphone app. If a household sees a 2 kW spike during peak hours, they can shift a dishwasher run to off‑peak, saving $0.10/kWh and easing grid stress.
learn.c3_text: India’s Smart Meter National Programme aims to install 250 million smart meters by 2027. Early pilots in Maharashtra cut residential peak demand by 5 % and enabled time‑of‑use tariffs for 2 million customers.
learn.stat2: IEA: Smart grids could reduce electricity losses by 2‑4 %
learn.stat4: Average latency of grid communication: <200 ms

learn.title: Perovskite Solar Cells
learn.intro: Perovskite materials promise record‑high efficiencies and low‑cost manufacturing, potentially surpassing silicon. Their rapid performance gains make them a focal point for next‑generation solar technology.
learn.c1_text: Imagine a crystal lattice that easily absorbs sunlight, like a sponge. Lab‑scale perovskite cells have reached 26 % efficiency—just a few years after the first report—compared with 23 % for top silicon modules.
learn.c2_text: Perovskite layers can be printed on flexible substrates using roll‑to‑roll processes, similar to newspaper printing. This could cut module costs to $0.20 per watt, half the price of conventional silicon panels.
learn.c3_text: The Indian Institute of Technology Delhi secured $5 million in government funding for perovskite pilot lines. A 2024 demonstration produced a 22 % efficient tandem cell (silicon + perovskite) on a rooftop in Hyderabad.
learn.stat2: Projected cost: $0.20/W for printed perovskite modules
learn.stat4: Tandem cells could reach >30 % efficiency by 2030

learn.title: Colour Codes of Hydrogen
learn.intro: Hydrogen’s colour labels—grey, blue, green—describe how it’s produced and its carbon footprint. Understanding these differences guides policy, investment, and the path to a zero‑carbon economy.
learn.c1_text: Grey hydrogen is made from natural‑gas steam methane reforming, releasing CO₂ directly. For every kilogram produced, roughly 10 kg of CO₂ are emitted, making it the most carbon‑intensive option.
learn.c2_text: Blue hydrogen uses the same reforming process but captures 90 % of the CO₂ for storage or utilization. This reduces emissions to about 1 kg CO₂ per kg H₂, but adds cost for capture equipment.
learn.c3_text: India’s National Hydrogen Mission (2023) targets 5 GW of green hydrogen electrolyzers by 2030, while supporting blue projects in Gujarat that combine natural‑gas reforming with carbon capture, aiming for 2 Mt CO₂ avoided annually.
learn.stat3: India: 1 GW green hydrogen capacity pledged (2024)

learn.title: Concentrated Solar Power (CSP)
learn.intro: CSP uses mirrors to concentrate sunlight, generating high‑temperature heat that drives turbines or stores energy as thermal fluid. This technology provides dispatchable solar power, complementing PV.
learn.c1_text: Imagine a field of mirrors focusing sunlight onto a tower. The concentrated heat (up to 400 °C) produces steam to spin a turbine, delivering electricity even after the sun sets, similar to a solar‑powered boiler.
learn.c2_text: Molten‑salt tanks store heat for 6–12 hours, allowing CSP plants to supply power during peak evening demand. A 100 MW CSP plant with 8 h storage can generate 800 MWh of dispatchable energy daily.
learn.c3_text: India’s 250 MW CSP plant in Rajasthan (operational 2025) uses parabolic troughs with 5 h molten‑salt storage, reducing reliance on coal for evening load and supporting the country’s 450 GW renewable target for 2030.
learn.stat2: CSP land use: ~2 ha per MW
